todaysEvents.summary: US-Israel move from ultimatum to limited power-grid and bridge strikes after the Apr 7 8pm ET deadline passes; no Kharg seizure yet, but kinetic pressure broadens materially (Apr 8).
Iran keeps the Tehran Toll Booth and bilateral safe-passage regime in place; Qatari LNG restrictions persist and AIS traffic remains roughly 95% below normal through Hormuz (Apr 8).
Brent jumps toward $118/bbl, JKM toward $23.6/MMBtu, and iTraxx Asia IG widens near 206bp as markets price a higher probability of follow-on strikes and prolonged Gulf disruption (Apr 8).

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.description: Post-deadline strikes remain limited to power-grid and bridge targets, creating room for Qatar/Pakistan mediation before Kharg or core export infrastructure is hit. Iran keeps symbolic toll authority but expands exemptions for commercial shipping. Brent retraces to $102-108/bbl; JKM eases to $18-20/MMBtu. Insurance remains impaired, but a narrow de-escalation channel re-opens if transit volumes recover measurably within days.

scenarios.label: Stress
scenarios.description: Limited strikes continue for several days, focused on grid, logistics, and command infrastructure rather than Kharg. Iran preserves the toll booth, bilateral routing, and selective tanker interference. Kuwait desalination and Qatari LNG disruption keep civilian and energy stress elevated while ceasefire talks drag. Brent stabilizes $110-135/bbl; JKM $21-25/MMBtu; iTraxx Asia IG 200-220bp.

scenarios.label: Tail
scenarios.description: Initial post-deadline strikes fail to restore transit and instead set up a second phase on Kharg, export terminals, and broader Gulf energy infrastructure. Iran responds with more missile and drone attacks on GCC civilian and military targets while keeping Hormuz commercially closed for non-exempt traffic. Ras Laffan remains structurally impaired; Saudi and UAE export reliability comes into question. Brent reaches $160-185/bbl, LNG spot markets seize, and ASEAN recession risk becomes acute.
